behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Which is correct about the lens accommodating for near vision?

options.A: It is increased in presbyopia
options.B: It reduces the dioptric power of the lens
options.C: It is due to ciliary-muscle relaxation and stretching of the zonular fibres
options.D: It is combined with miosis and convergence of the eyes (near reflex)

questions.explanation: The near reflex couples accommodation with miosis and convergence. For near focus the ciliary muscle CONTRACTS (zonules relax) and lens power increases; presbyopia reduces accommodation.

questions.stem: Which is correct about chemical burns of the cornea?

options.A: Alkalis cause more severe burns needing urgent specialist treatment
options.B: Acids penetrate deeper and destroy the eye's interior
options.C: Immediate irrigation with running water is absolutely contraindicated
options.D: No immediate care is needed; review at the next routine clinic
options.E: Treat with combined steroid+antibiotic drops and review in two weeks

questions.explanation: Alkalis penetrate deeper than acids and cause the most severe burns — irrigate immediately and refer urgently.

questions.stem: The anterior chamber is the space formed between:

options.A: the posterior iris and the anterior lens
options.B: the anterior iris and the posterior cornea
options.C: the posterior cornea and the anterior retina
options.D: the anterior cornea and the posterior iris
options.E: the posterior cornea and the anterior lens

questions.explanation: The anterior chamber lies between the posterior surface of the cornea and the anterior surface of the iris.

questions.stem: Main feature of epithelial HSV keratitis of the cornea?

options.A: Always self-limiting, no treatment needed
options.B: Improves with topical antibiotic drops
options.C: Worsens with topical corticosteroid drops
options.D: Presents with a characteristic amoeboid epithelial ulcer
options.E: Caused by herpes zoster from trigeminal fibres

questions.explanation: Topical steroids worsen HSV keratitis (dendritic → geographic/amoeboid). The typical primary lesion is a DENDRITIC, not amoeboid, ulcer.

questions.stem: Preseptal cellulitis of the right orbit in a 4-year-old:

options.A: No treatment beyond warm compresses and lid hygiene
options.B: Admission and IV antibiotics
options.C: Warm compresses and surgical incision if afebrile
options.D: Imaging and observation without treatment when no sinusitis
options.E: Oral antibiotics for a week without follow-up if low-grade fever

questions.explanation:
In a young child, periorbital cellulitis is admitted for IV antibiotics given the risk of progression to orbital cellulitis. The other options contain clear errors.

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): Mild preseptal cellulitis in older patients is often managed with oral antibiotics as an outpatient — the key here reflects cautious paediatric teaching plus elimination of the flawed options.

questions.stem: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy is mainly characterized by:

options.A: Soft retinal exudates
options.B: Diabetic macular oedema
options.C: Neovascularisation of the retina and/or optic disc
options.D: Ischaemia of retina and macula
options.E: Diabetic papillitis

questions.explanation: The defining feature is neovascularisation.

questions.stem: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is characterized by:

options.A: Visual (halo) phenomena
options.B: Stabbing focal pain on blinking and tearing
options.C: Normal IOP and deep pain
options.D: Reduced near vision with watery discharge
options.E: Conjunctival hyperaemia, corneal haze and a mid-dilated pupil

questions.explanation: Red eye, hazy cornea and a fixed mid-dilated pupil with very high IOP.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman developed thyroid eye disease 2 months ago. Which is correct?

options.A: Orbital decompression + thyroidectomy without prior medical therapy
options.B: IV steroids, which are less effective than oral steroids/NSAIDs
options.C: Unilateral exophthalmos and lid retraction
options.D: Lid retraction and mild exophthalmos are the main signs; restore thyroid function and monitor

questions.explanation: Early TED: lid retraction and mild proptosis — restore the euthyroid state and monitor. IV steroids are MORE effective than oral in active disease, and exophthalmos is usually bilateral.

questions.stem: A builder gets lime in the left eye and lids. Correct management?

options.A: Pressure dressing + antibiotic ointment for a day then outpatient review
options.B: Antibiotic drops + mydriatics for pain for a week
options.C: No concern for long-term eye health
options.D: Immediate copious irrigation, eyelid eversion to clean, urgent hospital referral
options.E: No treatment needed, self-limiting

questions.explanation: Alkali (lime) burn → immediate irrigation, evert the lids to remove particles, refer urgently.

questions.stem: Which condition needs immediate management?

options.A: Age-related macular degeneration
options.B: Hypertensive retinopathy
options.C: Vitreous detachment
options.D: Macular drusen

questions.explanation: Accelerated hypertensive retinopathy (with disc swelling) needs urgent blood-pressure control.

questions.stem: Degenerative entropion of the lower lid in an elderly cataract patient causes:

options.A: Tearing and foreign-body sensation with recurrent conjunctivitis from surface irritation
options.B: Cosmetic deformity managed by removing the lashes
options.C: Horizontal laxity and inward turning of the UPPER lid needing no treatment
options.D: Mechanical irritation that worsens the cataract without corneal damage
options.E: Definitive treatment is topical lubricant and antibiotics

questions.explanation: The inturned lid/lashes abrade the surface → FB sensation, tearing, recurrent conjunctivitis/keratitis. Definitive treatment is surgical.

questions.stem: Which is correct about rhegmatogenous retinal detachment?

options.A: A peripheral field defect (perceived shadow) that progressively extends toward central vision
options.B: Usually needs no treatment, stays stable with good VA
options.C: Peripheral vision is preserved at all stages
options.D: It coexists with macular degeneration
options.E: Pain is acute in the first hours

questions.explanation: RD gives a painless, progressively enlarging field defect/curtain that can reach central vision; it needs prompt treatment.

questions.stem: Which is correct about refractive errors?

options.A: Effortless distance focus on the retina can coexist with presbyopia
options.B: Pilocarpine causes presbyopia-like symptoms
options.C: Myopia is corrected with biconvex cylindrical lenses
options.D: Myopes need stronger presbyopic correction than emmetropes
options.E: Presbyopia is corrected with diverging lenses

questions.explanation: An emmetrope (effortless distance focus) still develops presbyopia with age.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old with stable −3D myopia shows:

options.A: Axial length usually shorter than normal
options.B: Correction with biconvex converging lenses as the image focuses behind the retina
options.C: Difficulty accommodating for near
options.D: Difficulty with both near and distance
options.E: Good correction with biconcave diverging lenses as the image focuses in front of the retina

questions.explanation: Myopia: longer axial length, image focused in front of the retina, corrected with biconcave (diverging) lenses.

questions.stem: Subconjunctival haemorrhage in an 86-year-old, found early morning. Likely cause:

options.A: Ocular hypertension and vitreous detachment
options.B: High-dose vitamin C in the last month
options.C: Dry eye from Sjögren's
options.D: Lower-limb venous thrombosis on long-term systemic anticoagulants
options.E: Advanced cataract with reduced VA

questions.explanation: In the elderly, systemic anticoagulation is a common cause of spontaneous subconjunctival haemorrhage.

questions.stem: Congenital glaucoma in an infant is mainly characterized by:

options.A: A larger-than-normal globe and cornea with initially diffuse corneal opacities
options.B: Focal corneal opacity with conjunctival neovascularisation
options.C: Tearing with seropurulent discharge
options.D: Deep pain with lens-space opacity
options.E: Advanced optic nerve damage

questions.explanation: Buphthalmos: enlarged globe/cornea, corneal oedema/opacities, tearing and photophobia.

questions.stem: Anterior uveitis is characterized by:

options.A: Eyelid oedema
options.B: Vitreous detachment
options.C: Raised IOP
options.D: Miosis and cells in the anterior chamber
options.E: Conjunctival discharge

questions.explanation: Anterior uveitis: ciliary-spasm miosis with cells and flare in the anterior chamber.

questions.stem: Which is correct about scleritis?

options.A: Always appears in young adults
options.B: Characterized by intense (deep, boring) pain
options.C: Is always anterior
options.D: Is usually idiopathic
options.E: The redness blanches with phenylephrine

questions.explanation: Scleritis = severe deep boring pain; it does NOT blanch with phenylephrine, can be posterior, and is often linked to systemic disease. (The 'young/idiopathic/blanching' descriptors fit episcleritis.)

questions.stem: The sclera of the eye:

options.A: Forms the posterior two-thirds of the globe
options.B: Is supplied by the anterior and posterior ciliary arteries
options.C: Is dehydrated and transparent
options.D: Helps produce aqueous humour
options.E: Dissolves the iris sphincter

questions.explanation:
The sclera/episclera receives blood from the ciliary arteries. It is opaque (the CORNEA is the transparent one), forms ~5/6 of the coat, and makes no aqueous.

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): Loose wording — the sclera proper is relatively avascular and is supplied via episcleral ciliary vessels, which makes (b) the best statement by elimination.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old with established orbital cellulitis usually has:

options.A: Orbital-apex syndrome treated with drops and oral NSAIDs
options.B: Chemosis and redness with restricted motility, treated with IV antibiotics
options.C: Lid redness with normal conjunctiva and good motility, no fever
options.D: Reduced VA and abnormal pupils needing no treatment
options.E: Managed without admission using oral antibiotics and monitoring

questions.explanation: Orbital cellulitis: chemosis, proptosis, restricted painful motility → admit for IV antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 53-year-old myope with good VA has sudden floaters and photopsias in the left eye for 3 days. Most likely:

options.A: Acute posterior vitreous detachment
options.B: Macular hole
options.C: Ocular migraine
options.D: Total retinal detachment
options.E: Optic neuritis

questions.explanation: Floaters + flashes + preserved VA = acute PVD; examine to exclude a retinal tear/detachment.

questions.stem: Which is correct about congenital ptosis?

options.A: Small bilateral ptosis is an immediate amblyopia risk up to age 3
options.B: It is only a cosmetic/refractive problem, repaired in adulthood
options.C: Surgical repair is needed when the visual axis is covered (amblyopia risk)
options.D: Managed temporarily with systemic cortisone
options.E: Usually has normal levator function and improves at puberty

questions.explanation: Operate when the lid covers the visual axis to prevent deprivation amblyopia.

questions.stem: Congenital cataract in neonates:

options.A: Is the only cause of leukocoria
options.B: Requires prompt management due to amblyopia risk
options.C: Treated with a contact lens until age 10
options.D: Is associated with congenital glaucoma
options.E: Is not urgent as vision is restored with glasses

questions.explanation: Needs early surgery to avoid deprivation amblyopia. (Retinoblastoma is another cause of leukocoria.)

questions.stem: Early herpes zoster of the left forehead and eyelids is characterized by:

options.A: Infection by a parvo/papova-group virus
options.B: Redness, vesicular skin lesions and pain
options.C: Regression after oral cortisone
options.D: An extensive dendritic corneal ulcer
options.E: Involvement of the second trigeminal branch

questions.explanation: HZO: painful dermatomal redness and vesicles. The forehead is the first (ophthalmic, V1) division — not the second.

questions.stem: A right Marcus Gunn pupil means:

options.A: Both pupils constrict (miosis) when the left eye is illuminated
options.B: Anisocoria when the left eye is illuminated
options.C: A lesion of the ipsilateral oculomotor nerve
options.D: Anisocoria when the right eye is illuminated
options.E: No pupil response when the left eye is illuminated

questions.explanation:
A right RAPD: the intact left eye drives a brisk consensual constriction; the defect shows as paradoxical dilation when the swinging light reaches the affected right eye.

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): A RAPD is an afferent (optic-nerve) defect seen on the swinging-flashlight test — it does not cause static anisocoria, which is why the other options are wrong.

questions.stem: A patient has a red eye with acute stabbing pain that worsens on blinking since this morning. Most likely:

options.A: Allergic conjunctivitis with chemosis
options.B: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
options.C: A foreign body trapped under the upper lid
options.D: Subconjunctival haemorrhage
options.E: Anterior uveitis with ciliary inflammation

questions.explanation: Pain that spikes with each blink suggests a sub-tarsal foreign body scratching the cornea.

questions.stem: Which is correct about raised intraocular pressure?

options.A: It is defined as exactly 22 mmHg
options.B: It is necessary for glaucomatous damage
options.C: It is synonymous with primary open-angle glaucoma
options.D: It is always present in normal-tension glaucoma
options.E: It is the most important predisposing factor for glaucoma

questions.explanation: Raised IOP is the chief (and treatable) risk factor — but glaucoma can occur at normal pressures (normal-tension glaucoma).

questions.stem: A 32-year-old with an inflamed chalazion of the right upper lid (2 days). Which is correct?

options.A: Soft-tissue inflammation needing immediate incision in all cases
options.B: Infected accessory sebaceous gland needing systemic antibiotics
options.C: Meibomian obstruction resolving with cryotherapy, massage, artificial tears
options.D: Meibomian gland infection, initially treated with warm compresses and antibiotic+steroid ointment
options.E: Larger version of a stye, treated only with warm compresses and systemic antibiotics

questions.explanation: An inflamed chalazion is managed initially with warm compresses and antibiotic±steroid ointment.

questions.stem: Which is true about strabismus?

options.A: In concomitant convergent strabismus the angle is constant in all gazes and there is exotropia
options.B: In infants with an undeveloped medial canthal ligament the corneal light reflex is off-centre
options.C: Correcting myopia can eliminate accommodative convergent strabismus
options.D: Exophoria is a permanent divergent strabismus
options.E: In right lateral-rectus paresis the angle between the axes increases on right gaze

questions.explanation: A paretic right lateral rectus (CN VI) → deviation worsens looking right. The others misuse terms: convergent = esotropia (not exotropia); pseudostrabismus has a CENTRAL reflex; accommodative esotropia comes from HYPEROPIA; exophoria is latent.

questions.stem: Increased corneal diameter (>12 mm):

options.A: Usually associated with systemic autoimmune disease
options.B: Is always pathological and causes blindness
options.C: In adults is accompanied by neurotrophic ulcers
options.D: In neonates is called buphthalmos when with epiphora, opacities and raised IOP
options.E: Is called megalocornea, is always opaque with low vision

questions.explanation: Buphthalmos = the enlarged cornea/globe of congenital glaucoma (epiphora, opacities, raised IOP). Megalocornea is usually clear and benign.

questions.stem: Which test is most important for early diagnosis of macular disease?

options.A: Distance visual acuity
options.B: Amsler grid
options.C: IOP measurement
options.D: Colour-vision test
options.E: Near visual acuity

questions.explanation: The Amsler grid detects early metamorphopsia/scotoma of macular disease.

questions.stem: Which is correct about a 78-year-old with senile cataract?

options.A: Accompanied by deep pain and conjunctival redness
options.B: A hypermature cataract never causes phacomorphic glaucoma
options.C: Rapid vision loss with photopsias
options.D: Vision always restored with corrective lenses
options.E: Progressive painless decrease in visual acuity

questions.explanation: Senile cataract = gradual painless VA loss; a hypermature lens CAN cause phacomorphic glaucoma.

questions.stem: Which is correct about acute angle-closure glaucoma?

options.A: Prognosis is better than open-angle glaucoma
options.B: The patient is usually asymptomatic
options.C: No need for immediate management
options.D: Usually appears in the 2nd decade
options.E: May present with intense nausea and vomiting

questions.explanation: AACG is a painful emergency, often with nausea/vomiting, in older (hyperopic) eyes.

questions.stem: Chronic open-angle glaucoma is a disease with:

options.A: Sudden painless loss of central vision
options.B: Progressive deterioration of optic nerve function
options.C: A link to sun exposure in youth
options.D: Symptoms tied to the rise in IOP
options.E: Prominent subjective complaints from field loss

questions.explanation: POAG progressively damages the optic nerve and is asymptomatic until advanced.

questions.stem: Paralytic strabismus — which is correct?

options.A: Requires immediate surgery
options.B: Covering the paralytic eye abolishes diplopia while covering the other eye keeps it
options.C: Children do not develop paralytic strabismus
options.D: Diplopia is worst for near and least for distance in all gazes
options.E: Diplopia increases on gaze toward the action of the paralytic muscle

questions.explanation: Diplopia is maximal in the direction of action of the weak muscle.

questions.stem: Claude-Bernard-Horner syndrome — which is correct?

options.A: Benign cause, so imaging is not recommended unless it persists
options.B: Caused by a parasympathetic lesion of the hemiface
options.C: Ptosis from increased Müller's-muscle action
options.D: Triad of miosis, upper-lid ptosis and facial anhidrosis
options.E: When congenital, no iris heterochromia occurs

questions.explanation: Horner's = miosis + partial ptosis + anhidrosis (a SYMPATHETIC lesion). Ptosis is from REDUCED Müller's action; congenital cases DO show iris heterochromia; the cause may be serious and warrants imaging.

questions.stem: Which corneal layer mainly maintains transparency?

options.A: Avascular stroma with collagen/elastic fibres
options.B: Endothelium with its Na⁺/K⁺ pump
options.C: Epithelium with sensory nerves
options.D: Descemet's membrane
options.E: Bowman's membrane

questions.explanation: The endothelial Na⁺/K⁺ pump dehydrates the stroma to keep the cornea clear.

questions.stem: Symptoms — which is correct?

options.A: Sudden loss of near vision with preserved distance from hyperopia in those over 45
options.B: Headache with ocular pain, red eye and a mid-dilated pupil may be retrobulbar neuritis
options.C: Headache worsening with eye movements may be a central retinal artery occlusion
options.D: Headache worse after sleep may be latent strabismus
options.E: Headache and reduced VA with pain on chewing may be temporal arteritis

questions.explanation: GCA: headache + jaw claudication + vision loss. (Red eye + mid-dilated pupil = acute glaucoma; pain on eye movement = optic neuritis.)

questions.stem: Active ocular toxoplasmosis:

options.A: Usually causes anterior uveitis
options.B: Is due to Candida
options.C: Is always congenital
options.D: Never needs treatment as it self-limits
options.E: Usually causes intense vitritis in the active phase

questions.explanation: Toxoplasma retinochoroiditis gives a focal lesion with overlying vitritis ('headlight in the fog'); it can be acquired and may need treatment near the macula/disc.

questions.stem: The uvea is anatomically divided into:

options.A: Cornea, sclera, retina
options.B: Iris, ciliary body, choroid
options.C: Iris, lens, retina
options.D: Iris, ciliary body, cyclic body
options.E: Cornea, iris, retina

questions.explanation: Uvea = iris + ciliary body + choroid.

questions.stem: An 83-year-old, 4 days after right cataract surgery, has a red eye, falling vision, intense pain and lid oedema. Diagnosis:

options.A: End-stage glaucoma
options.B: Postoperative endophthalmitis
options.C: Posterior uveitis
options.D: Postoperative macular oedema
options.E: Chronic recurrent scleritis

questions.explanation: Pain + falling vision + red eye days after cataract surgery = endophthalmitis until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Which is correct about aqueous humour?

options.A: Secreted by the epithelium of the ciliary processes
options.B: Drained via the tears
options.C: Facilitates lens accommodation
options.D: Produced by ultrafiltration from the ophthalmic artery
options.E: Is a major refractive component

questions.explanation: Aqueous is secreted by the ciliary-process epithelium and drained via the trabecular meshwork / canal of Schlemm.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old with postoperative right facial-nerve palsy. Which is correct?

options.A: Priority is correcting the brow/upper-lid droop from frontalis paralysis
options.B: VA is reduced from accommodation paralysis
options.C: It paralyses the levator, pupil sphincter and masseter
options.D: Priority is protecting the cornea from lagophthalmos with lubricants
options.E: Bell's phenomenon does not affect exposure keratitis

questions.explanation: CN VII palsy → lagophthalmos → protect the cornea with lubricants first.

questions.stem: The effective way to diagnose a corneal epithelial defect/ulcer is to:

options.A: Assess conjunctival redness and discharge
options.B: Do fundoscopy of the vitreous
options.C: Give artificial tears for a week
options.D: Ask about the pain and its nature
options.E: Instil fluorescein and view the cornea under cobalt-blue light

questions.explanation: Fluorescein + cobalt-blue light highlights the epithelial defect.

questions.stem: Which is correct about episcleritis?

options.A: Characterized by deep pain
options.B: Instilling 10% phenylephrine reduces the redness
options.C: In severe form it causes scleral perforation
options.D: It requires topical steroid therapy

questions.explanation: Episcleritis blanches with phenylephrine and is mild/self-limiting; deep pain and scleral perforation belong to scleritis.

questions.stem: Seasonal allergic conjunctivitis (spring) typically presents with:

options.A: Year-round mucopurulent discharge and lid redness
options.B: Redness/chemosis with itching and watery discharge, usually without reduced VA
options.C: Reduced VA from punctate corneal opacities
options.D: Unilateral redness with follicles and burning
options.E: Watery discharge and pain with limbal papillae (Trantas dots)

questions.explanation: Itching + watery discharge + chemosis, with normal vision. (Trantas dots belong to severe vernal disease.)

questions.stem: Leukocoria in a 6-month-old demands urgent investigation for:

options.A: Vitreous haemorrhage with possible detachment
options.B: Corneal ulcer with perforation risk
options.C: Conjunctivitis with purulent discharge
options.D: Retinoblastoma
options.E: Iridocyclitis with hypopyon

questions.explanation: Leukocoria in an infant = exclude retinoblastoma urgently.

questions.stem: For a conjunctivitis 'red eye', the usual truth is:

options.A: Bacterial conjunctivitis is common, often self-limiting and does not affect vision
options.B: Vision is markedly reduced without corneal involvement
options.C: The main sign is always mucopurulent discharge
options.D: Vitreous detachment is often the cause
options.E: It is a painful eye without reduced vision

questions.explanation: Conjunctivitis is common and usually self-limiting, with normal vision (no corneal involvement) and a gritty rather than truly painful eye.

questions.stem: Which is correct about visual fields?

options.A: A positive scotoma example is blindness from ischaemic optic neuropathy
options.B: A small peripheral arcuate loss is typical of early vitreous detachment
options.C: A bilateral central scotoma may be due to occipital-lobe damage
options.D: In bitemporal hemianopia there are positive scotomas in each temporal field
options.E: Vertical binasal hemianopia may be due to retinal vascular damage

questions.explanation: The occipital pole serves central/macular vision, so bilateral occipital damage can give bilateral central scotomata. (AION blindness is a NEGATIVE scotoma; arcuate loss is glaucoma.)

questions.stem: Which is correct about the eye's optics?

options.A: Myopia is unrelated to axial length
options.B: Lens thickening is achieved by ciliary-muscle relaxation
options.C: A hyperope can use accommodation to aid distance vision
options.D: In a myope at infinity, converging lenses shift the focus behind the retina
options.E: Myopes have greater accommodative ability by lens design

questions.explanation: Hyperopes accommodate even for distance to pull the focus onto the retina. (Lens thickening needs ciliary CONTRACTION; myopia is axial and corrected with diverging lenses.)

questions.stem: Bilateral conjunctival pterygium — the most correct prognosis:

options.A: Needs immediate removal because it becomes conjunctival carcinoma
options.B: Benign corneal covering that never causes problems beyond tearing
options.C: Usually causes chronic redness and progressive traction astigmatism that lowers VA
options.D: No surgery needed as it regresses with prolonged steroid drops
options.E: Causes itching and watery discharge treated with antibiotic drops

questions.explanation: A pterygium encroaching on the cornea induces astigmatism and reduces VA; surgery if it threatens the axis or is symptomatic.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old with acute left dacryocystitis (2 days). Which is correct?

options.A: Usually from a sinus infection, needs imaging
options.B: Obstruction at the canaliculi, treated by catheterisation
options.C: Inflammation of the ipsilateral lacrimal gland
options.D: Antibiotic drops and warm compresses when fever coexists
options.E: Obstruction below the lacrimal sac, treated initially with oral antibiotics and antibiotic drops

questions.explanation: Acute dacryocystitis = an infected sac from nasolacrimal-duct obstruction → systemic (oral) antibiotics.

questions.stem: The basic function of the ciliary body is:

options.A: By contracting, to relax the tension on the lens and regulate accommodation
options.B: Radial projections support the vitreous
options.C: Its relaxation changes corneal curvature for near vision
options.D: Its epithelium helps drain anterior-chamber aqueous
options.E: Its contraction causes mydriasis

questions.explanation: Ciliary-muscle contraction loosens the zonules → the lens rounds up for near focus (accommodation).

questions.stem: A 62-year-old with 12 years of type-2 diabetes (on tablets) has sudden painless blurring of the right eye with intense floaters. Most likely:

options.A: Vitreous haemorrhage
options.B: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
options.C: Advanced cataract
options.D: Anterior uveitis with hypopyon
options.E: Diabetic macular oedema

questions.explanation: Sudden painless loss + floaters in proliferative diabetic retinopathy = vitreous haemorrhage.

questions.stem: The most common intraocular tumour in adults is:

options.A: Choroidal malignant melanoma
options.B: Iris melanoma
options.C: Vitreous melanoma
options.D: Aqueous melanoma
options.E: Corneal melanoma

questions.explanation: Choroidal (uveal) melanoma.

questions.stem: In ocular-motor nerve lesions, which is correct?

options.A: Bell's phenomenon works in CN III palsy
options.B: CN III palsy gives a permanent down-and-out eye
options.C: Trochlear palsy causes ipsilateral ptosis
options.D: Abducens palsy causes a head turn to the opposite side
options.E: Trochlear palsy causes an ipsilateral head tilt

questions.explanation: Complete CN III palsy → a 'down-and-out' eye (unopposed lateral rectus and superior oblique). CN IV palsy has no ptosis and causes a CONTRALATERAL head tilt; CN VI palsy turns the face TOWARD the affected side.

questions.stem: Which is correct about the optic nerve?

options.A: In optic atrophy VA stays normal, only fields are affected
options.B: Compressive optic neuropathy presents with pain and normal pupils/fields
options.C: A 30-year-old with retrobulbar neuritis may have painful eye movements
options.D: Papilloedema from benign intracranial hypertension causes sudden rapid VA loss
options.E: Ischaemic disc oedema causes only a mild VA drop

questions.explanation: Retrobulbar (optic) neuritis classically gives painful eye movements in a young adult. Optic atrophy reduces VA; papilloedema preserves VA early; AION causes marked sudden loss.

questions.stem: An infant with left congenital nasolacrimal-duct obstruction has tearing and minimal discharge. Usually correct:

options.A: The duct is fully formed in utero; treat by catheterisation right at birth
options.B: Advise daily digital massage and surgery at age 5
options.C: If unresolved by ~9 months, probe the duct, preferably around 12 months
options.D: Treat only by dacryocystorhinostomy in adolescence
options.E: Treat with cortisone drops; it always heals

questions.explanation: Most cases resolve with massage by ~12 months; probing is offered around 12 months if not resolved.

questions.stem: A welder returns home in the evening with intense eye pain and tearing. Most likely:

options.A: No treatment needed, recovers next day
options.B: UV corneal burn (photokeratitis) with punctate keratitis
options.C: A metallic foreign body in the left cornea
options.D: Immediate treatment with topical anaesthetic drops for 2 days
options.E: Acute angle-closure glaucoma in the right eye

questions.explanation: Delayed UV photokeratitis with punctate keratitis; never treat with repeated topical anaesthetics.

questions.stem: In developed Western countries, the commonest cause of reduced VA in people over 60 is:

options.A: Chronic open-angle glaucoma
options.B: Diabetic retinopathy
options.C: Retinal detachment
options.D: Age-related macular degeneration
options.E: Posterior polar cataract

questions.explanation: AMD is the leading cause of vision loss in the elderly in developed countries.
